items.title: # Spot Trends Fast
items.content: Act as a market analyst. List the top five emerging consumer trends in the fitness industry for the next 12 months, using recent news, social media buzz, and sales data. Include a brief reason for each trend and a simple example of a product that could meet it.

items.title: # Competitor Snapshot
items.content: Pretend you are a business strategist. Compare three leading brands in the plant‑based snack market. Provide a table showing each brand's price range, main flavors, distribution channels, and one strength and weakness. Keep the info clear and easy to read.

items.title: # Customer Voice Map
items.content: Imagine you are conducting a survey for a new eco‑friendly water bottle. Generate ten short, open‑ended questions that reveal why people would buy or avoid it, focusing on price, design, sustainability, and daily use. Make each question simple and unbiased.

items.title: # Test Market Demand
items.content: Act as a market researcher. I have a business idea: [briefly describe idea]. List the top 5 questions I should ask potential customers, suggest where to find them, and explain what answers would indicate strong demand.

items.title: # Spot Problem Fit
items.content: Pretend you are a startup mentor. For my idea [brief description], identify the main problem it solves, describe the ideal user, and give three simple ways to prove the problem exists before building anything.

items.title: # Quick MVP Check
items.content: You are a product tester. Outline a 3‑day MVP experiment for my concept [brief description], list the minimum features needed, and tell me which metrics (e.g., sign‑ups, feedback score) prove the idea is worth pursuing.

items.title: # Cold Email Magic
items.content: Write a short cold outreach email for a SaaS product. Mention the prospect's recent blog post, explain one clear benefit, include a friendly question, and end with a short call to action. Keep it under 150 words and use a polite tone.

items.title: # Follow‑Up Boost
items.content: Create a follow‑up message to send 3 days after no reply. Restate the main value, add a new short case study, ask if they need more info, and suggest a quick 15‑minute call. Keep it concise, warm, and under 120 words.

items.title: # LinkedIn Connection Pitch
items.content: Generate a LinkedIn connection request note for a sales leader. Highlight a shared interest, mention how your solution can save their team time, and invite a brief chat. Limit to 300 characters and use a friendly professional tone.

items.title: # Write Winning Emails
items.content: Create a short, friendly email to a potential client. Mention their recent blog post, explain how our service can help them, and ask for a 15‑minute call. Keep it under 150 words, use a clear subject line, and end with a polite sign‑off.

items.title: # Craft Apology Note
items.content: Write a sincere apology email to a customer who received a delayed shipment. Acknowledge the mistake, explain briefly why it happened, offer a discount code, and promise faster service next time. Keep tone caring and under 120 words.

items.title: # Boost Referral Requests
items.content: Generate an email asking a satisfied client for a referral. Thank them for their business, highlight a recent success, and request an introduction to anyone who might need similar help. Include a short incentive and keep the message under 130 words.

items.title: # Ace Any Interview
items.content: Act as a friendly interview coach. Ask me common questions for a software engineer role, then give brief feedback on my answers and one tip to improve each response. Keep the tone supportive and use simple language.

items.title: # Mock Interview Sprint
items.content: Run a 5‑minute mock interview for a marketing manager position. Pose one question at a time, wait for my reply, then grade my answer on clarity, relevance, and confidence, and suggest one concrete example I could add.

items.title: # STAR Story Builder
items.content: Help me craft a STAR (Situation, Task, Action, Result) story for the question: "Tell me about a time you solved a tough problem." Ask for each part, then combine them into a concise 2‑sentence answer.

items.title: # Perfect Cover Letter
items.content: Write a cover letter for a [Job Title] at [Company Name]. Include a brief intro, two key achievements that match the job description, and a closing that shows enthusiasm. Use a professional tone, keep it under 300 words, and format with proper salutations.

items.title: # Tailor Your Pitch
items.content: Create a customized cover letter for a marketing coordinator role at a startup. Highlight my experience with social media campaigns, data‑driven results, and teamwork. Mention why I love fast‑paced environments and end with a call to discuss how I can add value.

items.title: # Stand Out Quickly
items.content: Generate a concise cover letter (150‑200 words) for an entry‑level software engineer position. Start with a hook about my passion for coding, list one project that used Python and teamwork, and close with a confident statement about contributing to the company's tech goals.

items.title: # Perfect Resume Summary
items.content: Write a concise, 3‑sentence professional summary for a resume. Include the job title, years of experience, top skill, and a measurable achievement. Make it sound confident and tailored to a marketing manager role.

items.title: # Action‑Verb Bullet Builder
items.content: Create five resume bullet points for a software engineer. Start each with a strong action verb, mention the technology used, and add a result with numbers (e.g., % increase, users served). Keep each bullet under 20 words.

items.title: # Keyword Optimizer
items.content: List the top 10 keywords that an applicant tracking system would look for in a data analyst resume. Include both hard skills and soft skills, and explain briefly how to weave each keyword into the work experience section.
